On the edge of the world, the trickster Ahn kept people laughing while stealing their danger. He wore a grin of feathers and a wallet of shadows. At first he offered bargains: a cure for hunger, a path to fortune, a song that would summon rain. People trusted his charm and paid him with mornings. He sent others forward; he kept three-quarters.

One clever betrayal worked only once: he sold a map to a famine-struck village, promising springs if they followed his instructions. They did, and the map led not to water but to a well that vanished when they drew near. The town became empty of hope, but the map vanished with it, and Ahn laughed as the harvest failed elsewhere.

Then detection arrived: a priest of the mountains, who heard the river sigh and found that every fortune the trickster promised lacked a seed of truth. The priest summoned the ancestors, who set a judgment that would not be ignored. Ahn was bound to trade forever, a price he paid in breath.

The people learned to test promises, and the cycle ended when the trickster’s breath wore thin and refused to betray again. Silence grew wiser than theft.
